_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
The EPA HD Phase 3 GHG rule — and the subsequent February 2026 federal rescission — sit squarely in the vehicle-manufacturer compliance layer of the heavy-duty freight supply chain, not at the freight forwarder layer. The rule's substantive obligations were imposed on original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and engine makers: they were required to certify vehicles to CO2 emission intensity standards (measured in grams CO2 per ton-mile of freight moved) and to participate in the Averaging, Banking, and Trading (ABT) program. Freight forwarders, as transport arrangers rather than vehicle manufacturers or registered fleet operators managing their own certificated vehicles under EPA's program, had no direct EPA registration, certification, or reporting obligations under Phase 3.

However, the workspace is indirectly and materially exposed in the following ways. First, the workspace procures road transport capacity — primarily over-the-road Class 8 tractor-trailers and vocational vehicles for delivery, event logistics, fine art moves, and humanitarian supply chain movements — from carriers whose fleet specifications and unit costs are directly shaped by OEM compliance pathways. Second, major shipper clients in the live events, luxury goods, film/TV, and high-value automotive verticals increasingly embed fleet GHG benchmarks, ZEV preferences, or Scope 3 transport emission reporting requirements in their freight contracts, effectively transmitting regulatory-equivalent obligations downstream to freight forwarders. Third, the workspace's own Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation) and Category 9 (downstream transportation) emissions inventories, if reported voluntarily or under future SEC or state climate disclosure mandates, will be shaped by carrier fleet compliance with Phase 3 standards or their successor.

Role placement requiring legal confirmation: The workspace should confirm with counsel whether any ground transport arrangements — particularly any dedicated fleet, vehicle lease, or operating agreement under which the workspace directs vehicle specifications or procurement — creates a de facto "fleet operator" status that could trigger OEM-facing compliance obligations or state-level fleet purchase mandates (e.g., California ACF large private fleet thresholds). This determination is jurisdiction-specific and fact-dependent.

*Source: Final Rule: Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standards for Heavy-Duty Vehicles – Phase 3, U.S. EPA, March 29, 2024 (published Federal Register April 22, 2024, 89 FR 29440). https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions-vehicles-and-engines/final-rule-greenhouse-gas-emissions-standards-heavy-duty. See also: Final Rule: Rescission of the Greenhouse Gas Endangerment Finding and Motor Vehicle Greenhouse Gas Emission Standards Under the Clean Air Act, U.S. EPA, February 12, 2026. https://www.epa.gov/regulations-emissions-vehicles-and-engines/final-rule-rescission-greenhouse-gas-endangerment*
